title: Slab-built clay picture frame
subtitle: A flat frame pressed from two slabs with a cut-out window
excerpt: A clay picture frame built from two slabs: a back panel and a front panel with the window cut out. Air-dry clay, no kiln required. The finished frame sits on a shelf or hangs on a wall and holds a small photograph or print.
sourceNotes: Slab-building technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to join.

content.text: A clay picture frame is a two-piece slab construction: a solid back panel and a front panel with the window opening cut from its centre. The two pieces join when soft, dry flat, and form a channel at the back for sliding in a small photograph or print. No kiln required.
to 6 mm thickness, each about 15 cm by 12 cm. Let them rest 5 minutes on the board.
2. Add texture to the front slab
Press stamps, lace, or leaves into the front slab while it is still soft. Work around the area where the window will be cut.

Mark a rectangle or oval in the centre of the front slab, leaving at least 25 mm of clay on all sides as the frame border. Cut the window out cleanly with a fettling knife. Remove the cut piece. Smooth the inner edge with a damp finger.

Score the perimeter of the back slab and the matching perimeter of the front slab with a needle tool. Brush slurry on both scored areas. Press the front slab onto the back slab, aligning the edges. Leave the top edge open: this becomes the slot for sliding in the photograph. Press the three joined edges firmly and wipe away excess slurry.
Roll a thick coil, about 15 mm in diameter and 5 cm long. Score the back of the frame at the base and press the coil on as an angled foot. Let it firm to
Dry flat on the board under light plastic cover for the first 6 hours. Full drying takes two to three days for the doubled wall thickness. Once bone-dry, seal with PVA or paint then seal.

items.symptom: Front and back slabs separate at the edges
items.cause: Slabs were at different drying stages or were not scored before joining
items.fix: Rebind with fresh slurry while still damp; press firmly and hold for 30 seconds

items.cause: Dried too quickly or one side drier than the other
items.fix: Weight the frame face-down on a flat board as it dries; a sheet of glass or ceramic tile works well
